1:
"And an angel of the LORD came up from Gilgal to Bochim, and said, I made you to go up out of Egypt, and have brought you unto the land which I sware unto your fathers; and I said, I will never break my covenant with you." The witnesses note the angel of the LORD coming up from Gilgal to Bochim, declaring God's faithful covenant against Israel's unfaithfulness — the crux of the unbreakable covenant of God, set against the broken faith of his people. On "I made you to go up out of Egypt... and I said, I will never break my covenant with you," interpreters expound the message. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the faithful covenant — "and an angel of the LORD came up from Gilgal to Bochim, and said, I made you to go up out of Egypt, and have brought you unto the land which I sware unto your fathers; and I said, I will never break my covenant with you": the angel, speaking in God's name, rehearses his mercies and his unbroken faithfulness; the truth that God's mercies bind his people to obedience, and his faithfulness aggravates their sin, the covenant kept by him and broken by them, "I am the LORD, I change not," Malachi 3:6; the figure — the Angel of the covenant, the LORD himself pleading with his people, fulfilled in Christ the Mediator who keeps the covenant on God's part, "a faithful high priest". The figure of the unbreakable covenant of God, set against the broken faith of his people.

On "and an angel of the LORD came up from Gilgal to Bochim, and said, I made you to go up out of Egypt, and have brought you unto the land which I sware unto your fathers," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the rehearsal of mercies. Keil and Ellicott note the angel of the LORD coming from Gilgal to Bochim and rehearsing God's mercies, the deliverance from Egypt and the gift of the land sworn to the fathers (the faithful covenant, Malachi 3:6) — the message (I brought you up).

On "and I said, I will never break my covenant with you," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the unbroken faithfulness. Calvin and Gill note God declaring he had never broken his covenant, his perfect faithfulness set against Israel's unfaithfulness, the breach wholly theirs, fulfilled in Christ the Angel of the covenant who keeps it on God's part (the faithfulness of God, 2 Timothy 2:13) — the figure (the Angel of the covenant pleading with his people); the providence (the message, the charge of their disobedience to follow, vv.2). The verse displays the angel declaring God's faithful covenant against Israel's unfaithfulness.

The witnesses agree the substance: the angel of the LORD rehearses God's mercies and his unbroken faithfulness, that the breach lay with Israel (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the faithful covenant: I will never break my covenant, God faithful though his people are faithless (2 Timothy 2:13), unchanging in his covenant (Malachi 3:6), a figure of Christ the Angel and Mediator of the covenant who keeps it on God's part, his faithfulness the ground of hope and the measure of guilt. The verse displays the faithful-covenant crux. The angel declaring God's faithful covenant against Israel's unfaithfulness displays the crux: I made you to go up out of Egypt... and I said, I will never break my covenant with you (2 Timothy 2:13; Malachi 3:6) — the unbreakable covenant of God set against the broken faith of his people, his perfect faithfulness keeping every word while they broke theirs, a figure of Christ the faithful Mediator of the covenant. The verse displays the charge that they had not obeyed God's voice (vv.2).

10:
"And also all that generation were gathered unto their fathers: and there arose another generation after them, which knew not the LORD, nor yet the works which he had done for Israel." The witnesses note that when the eyewitness generation died, another arose that knew not the LORD nor his works — the crux of the failure to hand down the knowledge of God, and the apostasy that follows a generation's forgetting. On "there arose another generation after them, which knew not the LORD, nor yet the works which he had done for Israel," interpreters expound the decline. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the generation that forgot God — "and also all that generation were gathered unto their fathers: and there arose another generation after them, which knew not the LORD, nor yet the works which he had done for Israel": when the generation that had seen God's works died, a new one arose ignorant of him; the truth that the fathers are charged to teach their children the works of God, lest the generation to come forget him, "that the generation to come might know them... who should arise and declare them to their children: that they might set their hope in God, and not forget the works of God," Psalm 78:6-7; the figure — the peril of every age that does not teach its children the faith, the candle of the knowledge of God going out in a single generation's neglect. The figure of the failure to hand down the knowledge of God, and the apostasy that follows a generation's forgetting.

12:
"And they forsook the LORD God of their fathers, which brought them out of the land of Egypt, and followed other gods, of the gods of the people that were round about them, and bowed themselves unto them, and provoked the LORD to anger." The witnesses note that Israel forsook the LORD their deliverer and followed the gods of the nations around them — the crux of the great apostasy, the heart's turning from the living God to the idols of the world. On "they forsook the LORD God of their fathers... and followed other gods... and provoked the LORD to anger," interpreters expound the apostasy. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the forsaking of God — "and they forsook the LORD God of their fathers, which brought them out of the land of Egypt, and followed other gods, of the gods of the people that were round about them, and bowed themselves unto them, and provoked the LORD to anger": Israel abandoned the God who had redeemed them and turned to the idols of the surrounding nations; the truth that apostasy provokes the just anger of God, who is jealous for his honour and will not give his glory to another, "they provoked him to jealousy with strange gods," Deuteronomy 32:16; the figure — the soul that forsakes God for the idols of the world, the spiritual adultery that leaves the husband for strangers, the heart's affections given to the creature. The figure of the great apostasy, the heart's turning from the living God to the idols of the world.

16:
"Nevertheless the LORD raised up judges, which delivered them out of the hand of those that spoiled them." The witnesses note that, despite Israel's sin, the LORD raised up judges to deliver them — the crux of the sovereign mercy of God, who sends deliverers to his undeserving people. On "the LORD raised up judges, which delivered them out of the hand of those that spoiled them," interpreters expound the mercy. The figure of the sovereign mercy of God, who sends deliverers to his undeserving people.

18:
"And when the LORD raised them up judges, then the LORD was with the judge, and delivered them out of the hand of their enemies all the days of the judge: for it repented the LORD because of their groanings by reason of them that oppressed them and vexed them." I will not execute the fierceness of mine anger," Hosea 11:9; the figure — the compassion of God in Christ, who is touched with the feeling of our infirmities and moved by the groaning of his people, "like as a father pitieth his children, so the LORD pitieth them that fear him," Psalm 103:13. The figure of the deepening sin of a people unchanged by deliverance, the obstinacy that returns to evil.

God's resolve to leave the nations Joshua had left displays the foe left by God's just judgment: I also will not henceforth drive out any from before them of the nations which Joshua left when he died (Judges 2:3) — God resolving to leave the nations remaining at Joshua's death, the foe left as the just consequence of Israel's sin. The verse displays that the nations were left to prove Israel (vv.22).
22:
"That through them I may prove Israel, whether they will keep the way of the LORD to walk therein, as their fathers did keep it, or not." The very brief verse notes that the nations were left to prove Israel. The grammatical voices note the testing. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "through them I may prove Israel, whether they will keep the way of the LORD to walk therein, as their fathers did keep it, or not" — God left the nations to test whether Israel would keep his way; the that through them I may prove Israel (to prove Israel); the whether they will keep the way of the LORD to walk therein, as their fathers did keep it, or not (whether they would keep his way); the figure (the nations left as a test of Israel's faithfulness, the trial of obedience; the truth that God left the foe to prove whether Israel would walk in his way, the test of the heart, Deuteronomy 8:2; the figure of the trial that proves the heart, the foe a test of faithfulness; the proving of Israel; the test of obedience, vv.21). The verse holds together the proving of Israel.

On "That through them I may prove Israel," the witnesses note the proving. Gill notes God proving Israel by the nations (the test, Deuteronomy 8:2) — the proving (through them).

On "whether they will keep the way of the LORD to walk therein, as their fathers did keep it, or not," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the test: "whether they will keep the way of the LORD to walk therein, as their fathers did keep it, or not" — the test whether Israel would keep God's way (the trial); the figure (the trial that proves the heart); the providence (the test, the nations left and the book's narrative to follow, vv.23). The verse displays the nations left to prove Israel.

23:
"Therefore the LORD left those nations, without driving them out hastily; neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua." The very brief verse notes the LORD leaving the nations, not driving them out hastily. The grammatical voices note the close. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD left those nations, without driving them out hastily; neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua" — God left the nations, not driving them out quickly, to be a test and scourge; the therefore the LORD left those nations, without driving them out hastily (the nations left); the neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua (not given to Joshua); the figure (God leaving the nations as a test, the foe remaining by his purpose; the truth that the nations were left, not given to Joshua, by God's design to prove Israel, Judges 3:1; the figure of the foe left in the land by God's purpose, the test of the people; the nations left; the close of the summary, vv.21-22). The verse closes the programmatic summary of the book. The verse displays the nations left as the standing test and scourge of Israel through the days of the judges, the foe by which God would prove and chasten his people. The verse displays the providence of God that overrules even Israel's sin, leaving the nations to be the means of his discipline and the occasion of his repeated deliverances.

On "Therefore the LORD left those nations, without driving them out hastily," the witnesses note the nations left. Gill notes God leaving the nations as a test (the purpose, Judges 3:1) — the nations (left).

On "neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the design: "neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua" — the nations not given to Joshua, by God's design (the purpose); the figure (the foe left by God's purpose); the providence (the close, the narrative of the judges to follow, ch.3). The verse displays the LORD leaving the nations, not driving them out hastily.

The close displays the foe left in the land by God's purpose. The verse displays the nations: a test. The verse displays the design: God's. The verse displays the summary: ended. The LORD leaving the nations, not driving them out hastily, displays the foe left by God's purpose: the LORD left those nations, without driving them out hastily; neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua (Judges 3:1) — God leaving the nations, not driving them out quickly, to be the standing test and scourge of Israel by his design. The verse closes the summary, before the narrative of the judges begins (ch.3).
